local_names.romanized_local: Milo Panas (Malay); Mei lu re yin (Mandarin)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Milo Hot; closest match is chocolate malt beverage powder (FOODON_03301776). Closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cocoa and chocolate-based beverages; used as closest defensible category for Milo Hot malted chocolate drink powder. Closest_parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for malted chocolate drink powder; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

preparation_method: dissolved in hot water or hot milk

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard mug of Milo Hot is approximately 250 g, typically prepared by dissolving 3 heaped teaspoons (approximately 30 g) of Milo powder in hot water or hot milk.

nutrient_source.title: Milo Hot — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database (cr_id G00010638)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Milo Hot (per 100 g powder) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010638, confidence 0.95). HPB is the approved primary regional source for Singaporean food records. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.04 g/100 g) are reported in the input record from a secondary label-derived source at lower confidence (0.7); these values are consistent with the published Nestlé Milo Singapore product nutrition information panel and are retained as supporting data. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from the HPB Foodo approved source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Milo (Nestlé) dissolved in water
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database records a GI of 55 for Nestlé Milo dissolved in water (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008, Table A2). This is the same product formulation as Milo Hot powder prepared as a hot drink. GI 55 is classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per 30 g powder serving (27.9 g) / 100 = 15.3. Carbohydrate per serving derived from HPB per-100g value (93.0 g/100 g × 0.30 = 27.9 g per 30 g powder serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (72.7 g/100 g) classifying this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, combined with elevated sodium (530 mg/100 g) in the powder concentrate
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Milo Hot due to its extremely high sugar load (72.7 g/100 g powder), which independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, and its high sodium content (530 mg/100 g); switch to Milo Kosong (unsweetened variant) or plain low-fat milk to eliminate both the sugar and sodium burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high sugar (72.7 g/100 g) and carbohydrate (93 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre, producing rapid postprandial glucose excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Milo Hot powder is contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its sugar content of 72.7 g/100 g and carbohydrate content of 93 g/100 g with 0 g dietary fibre; a standard 30 g serving delivers approximately 22 g sugar, nearly exhausting the entire daily added sugar allowance, and should be replaced with Milo Kosong or unsweetened cocoa powder with low-fat milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high added sugar content (72.7 g/100 g) promoting dyslipidaemia, hypertriglyceridaemia, and weight gain; sodium 530 mg/100 g further elevates cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Milo Hot is not recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its very high sugar content (72.7 g/100 g), which promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and adverse lipid profiles, combined with high sodium (530 mg/100 g) in the powder; patients should switch to unsweetened cocoa-based alternatives with low-fat milk and no added sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory sugar load (72.7 g/100 g) far exceeding the 15 g/100 g pro-inflammatory threshold, outweighing any minor cocoa polyphenol benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Milo Hot is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as its sugar content of 72.7 g/100 g drives systemic inflammatory mediator production, outweighing any minor anti-inflammatory benefit from cocoa polyphenols; switch to unsweetened cocoa powder with low-fat milk and no added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Milo Hot is a popular malted chocolate drink powder produced by Nestlé, widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, and Southeast Asia. It is prepared by dissolving approximately 3 heaped teaspoons (30 g) of the powder in hot water or hot milk to produce a warm, sweet, chocolatey beverage with a distinctive malt flavour. The powder is an ultra-processed formulation containing malt extract, sugar, cocoa powder, skimmed milk powder, and a range of added vitamins and minerals including calcium (909 mg/100 g), iron (16.4 mg/100 g), and magnesium (218 mg/100 g). Milo is deeply embedded in Singaporean food culture and is commonly consumed at breakfast, as a school canteen drink, and at kopitiams (traditional coffee shops). It is available hot or iced (Milo Ais or Milo Dinosaur). Nutritionally, the powder is extremely high in sugar (72.7 g/100 g) and carbohydrates (93 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre, making it a high-glycaemic, sugar-dense product. Despite its fortification with micronutrients, the very high sugar and sodium (530 mg/100 g) content make it clinically unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index of Milo dissolved in water is 55 (low GI per University of Sydney GI Database), but the glycemic load per standard 30 g powder serving is approximately 15.3 due to the high carbohydrate density. Protein and total fat content per 100 g of the powder are reported as 0 g by HPB Singapore, reflecting the product formulation at the powder level where these macros fall below the reportable threshold; saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.04 g/100 g) are present at low levels. Milo Kosong (no added sugar variant) is the recommended alternative for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in Milo?
Is Milo good for high blood pressure?
Is Milo suitable for heart patients?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010638, confidence 0.95), which is the approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat and trans fat values from product label data are consistent with published Nestlé Milo Singapore nutrition information panel. GI value of 55 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008) for Nestlé Milo dissolved in water — same product formulation.
notes: ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010638); protein contribution from malt and milk solids falls below the reportable threshold in the HPB dataset at the powder level. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; total fat is below the reportable threshold at the powder level per HPB data, though saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.04 g/100 g) are present at trace levels from product label data. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Singapore Foodo database for Milo Hot powder as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Milo Hot (G00010638) as of 2026; while Milo is known to be fortified with several vitamins and minerals, the specific per-100g values for these nutrients are not available in the approved HPB source used for this record. potassium_mg reported as 0.0 per HPB Foodo database — confirmed zero. ONTOLOGY NOTE: FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology entry exists for Milo Hot specifically. PORTION NOTE: The input record lists a portion of 250 g (1 standard mug/cup); this is interpreted as the prepared beverage weight (powder dissolved in hot water or milk), not the powder weight alone. GI NOTE: GI of 55 is sourced from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) for Nestlé Milo dissolved in water; this is the same product formulation and is a defensible direct reference. GL per serving = 55 × 27.9 / 100 = 15.3. SOURCE COMPLIANCE NOTE: All nutrient data are drawn from HPB Singapore Foodo (approved primary regional source). Saturated fat and trans fat supplementary values are from product label data retained as supporting evidence consistent with the HPB-sourced record; no forbidden sources are used as primary evidence.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced with HPB as primary nutrient source.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types to 'closest_parent'; corrected gi_category to 'low' for GI 55; added missing romanized alias to flat aliases array.

Iteration 3: Replaced forbidden/non-compliant nutrient_source citation with fully compliant HPB Singapore Foodo reference (cr_id G00010638) as sole primary source. Replaced non-compliant gi_source with University of Sydney GI Database citation (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) for Nestlé Milo dissolved in water. Removed any reference to delivery platforms or non-approved sources as primary evidence. Updated notes with SOURCE COMPLIANCE NOTE. Corrected GL calculation to 15.3. Replaced romanized alias 'Měi lù rè yǐn' with ASCII-safe 'Mei lu re yin' for consistency across aliases and aliases_by_language.
